_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella overview of organ-specific fibroses. Cross-referenced ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT 2022 IPF guideline (Raghu et al PMID 35486072), AASLD 2023 NAFLD/MASLD guidance (Rinella PMID 37363821), KDIGO 2024 CKD guideline, INBUILD and INPULSIS nintedanib trials, and CAPACITY/ASCEND pirfenidone trials. Fibroscan/MR elastography thresholds verified against Baveno VII and EASL clinical practice guidelines on noninvasive tests.

h1Title: Fibrosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Fibrosis is the pathological replacement of normal organ tissue with stiff, collagen-rich scar laid down by activated myofibroblasts after sustained injury. It is the final common pathway of chronic damage in the lungs, liver, kidneys, heart, skin, and bone marrow, and is implicated in roughly 45% of deaths in the industrialized world when ischemic and chronic organ-failure causes are combined. Presentation depends on the organ scarred: progressive breathlessness in pulmonary fibrosis, jaundice and ascites in advanced hepatic fibrosis, falling glomerular filtration in renal fibrosis, and stiff non-compliant chambers in cardiac fibrosis. Until 2014, fibrosis was viewed as irreversible; antifibrotic drugs pirfenidone and nintedanib now slow lung fibrosis progression, and aggressive removal of the inciting injury can reverse hepatic and renal fibrosis at earlier stages.

keyStats.prevalence: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis affects 13-20 per 100,000 adults (Raghu 2022); advanced liver fibrosis affects roughly 1.5% of US adults (NHANES Fibroscan data 2017-2018)
keyStats.demographics: Pulmonary fibrosis: men over 60, ex-smokers; hepatic fibrosis: adults with MASLD, chronic hepatitis B/C, alcohol use; cardiac fibrosis: post-MI, hypertensive, and amyloid patients
keyStats.avgAge: IPF typically diagnosed at 65-75 years; advanced liver fibrosis identified at 50-70 years; cardiac fibrosis accumulates from middle age onward
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 3 million people worldwide live with IPF; over 100 million adults globally have significant liver fibrosis from MASLD (Lazarus 2022)

definition: Fibrosis (ICD-10: J84.10 for unspecified pulmonary fibrosis; cross-coded as K74.6 hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis, N26 unspecified renal sclerosis, I42.5 endomyocardial fibrosis) is the excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix — mainly type I and type III collagen, fibronectin, and proteoglycans — in response to chronic tissue injury. The driver cell is the myofibroblast, derived from resident fibroblasts, epithelial cells undergoing epithelial-mesenchymal transition, pericytes, and circulating fibrocytes. Sustained injury activates TGF-β, PDGF, CTGF, and the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, while failure of fibrinolysis and senescence of regenerative epithelium prevent normal repair. The result is progressive replacement of functional parenchyma with mechanically stiff scar that distorts architecture, impairs perfusion, and drives organ failure. Fibrosis is staged by organ — METAVIR F0-F4 in liver, GAP score in IPF, eGFR-based CKD stages with biopsy-defined interstitial fibrosis tubular atrophy in kidney. Management is shared across specialties: pulmonology and interstitial lung disease teams lead pulmonary fibrosis; hepatology manages hepatic fibrosis; nephrology handles renal fibrosis; cardiology with cardiac MRI expertise leads cardiac fibrosis; and dermatology or rheumatology manages dermal fibrosis in scleroderma.

typesClassification.description: Progressive scarring of the lung interstitium. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is the prototype, with usual interstitial pneumonia pattern on HRCT (subpleural, basal reticulation and honeycombing). Other forms include connective tissue disease-associated, hypersensitivity pneumonitis-related, and drug-induced pulmonary fibrosis.

Scarring of the liver from chronic hepatitis B or C, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH/NASH), alcohol, autoimmune hepatitis, or biliary disease. Staged F0 to F4 by METAVIR; F4 is cirrhosis. Early stages are reversible if the driver is removed; cirrhosis is largely structural and irreversible.

Interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IFTA) plus glomerulosclerosis are the histological hallmarks of progressive chronic kidney disease. Drivers include diabetes, hypertension, glomerulonephritis, obstruction, and transplant rejection. Once IFTA exceeds 50% on biopsy, progression to kidney failure is near-certain.

Replacement fibrosis after myocardial infarction or focal injury, and reactive interstitial fibrosis in hypertensive heart disease, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and amyloidosis. Increases chamber stiffness, drives heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and creates arrhythmia substrates.

Rare fibroinflammatory disease of the retroperitoneum that encases the aorta, ureters, and adjacent structures. Two thirds are idiopathic (chronic periaortitis, often IgG4-related); a third are secondary to drugs (ergot derivatives, methysergide), malignancy, or radiation.

Skin and internal organ fibrosis driven by autoimmune systemic sclerosis (scleroderma). Skin thickening progresses from fingers proximally; internal involvement includes interstitial lung disease, renal crisis, and gastrointestinal dysmotility.

Includes tropical endomyocardial fibrosis (I42.5), nephrogenic systemic fibrosis from older gadolinium contrast agents, radiation-induced fibrosis of treated tissues, and bone marrow myelofibrosis (a clonal hematologic disorder).

primarySymptoms: Progressive exertional breathlessness in pulmonary fibrosis, typically worsening over 6-24 months and out of proportion to chest imaging at first.
Dry, persistent cough — the second cardinal feature of IPF, present in over 80% of patients and often refractory to standard antitussives.
Fine bibasal inspiratory crackles on chest auscultation, described as ‘Velcro-like’, present in roughly 90% of IPF cases by the time of diagnosis.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance across all organ-specific fibroses, often the earliest patient-reported symptom.
Right upper-quadrant discomfort, abdominal distension, and easy bruising in advanced hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis.
Lower-limb edema and reduced urine output as renal fibrosis progresses toward stage 4-5 chronic kidney disease.
Exertional chest tightness, palpitations, and orthopnea in cardiac fibrosis with heart failure preserved ejection fraction.
Skin tightening that starts at the fingers (sclerodactyly) and progresses proximally in systemic sclerosis-associated fibrosis.
Clubbing of the fingers, observed in roughly 50% of patients with established IPF and a smaller proportion of those with chronic hepatic disease.
Unintentional weight loss and muscle wasting (sarcopenia) common across late-stage organ fibroses.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained dry cough lasting more than 8 weeks in an adult over 50, especially an ex-smoker
Mildly raised liver enzymes or an incidentally noted bright liver on ultrasound in someone with central obesity or type 2 diabetes
Persistently low-grade proteinuria (urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio >30 mg/g) or a gentle downward eGFR trend over consecutive checks
New shortness of breath when climbing stairs that did not previously cause symptoms
Family history of pulmonary fibrosis, telomere-syndrome features (premature graying, cytopenias), or unexplained adult-onset cryptogenic cirrhosis
emergencySigns: Sudden worsening of breathlessness within days in established pulmonary fibrosis — a possible acute exacerbation of IPF with 50% in-hospital mortality, requiring urgent assessment
Hematemesis or melena in known hepatic fibrosis or cirrhosis — likely variceal bleeding, a life-threatening emergency
Confusion, drowsiness, or asterixis in cirrhosis — possible hepatic encephalopathy requiring inpatient care
Acute reduction in urine output, leg swelling, and breathlessness in chronic kidney disease — possible uremia or fluid overload
Syncope, ventricular arrhythmia, or new heart failure in patients with known cardiac fibrosis — immediate cardiology assessment

causes.description: Repeated micro-injury to alveolar epithelium in a genetically susceptible aging lung leads to aberrant repair, fibroblast foci, and progressive scarring. Median survival without antifibrotics is 3-5 years from diagnosis. Roughly 13-20 per 100,000 adults are affected.

causes.cause: Chronic viral hepatitis and metabolic liver disease
causes.description: Hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) trigger stellate-cell activation and progressive collagen deposition. MASH is now the leading global cause of advanced hepatic fibrosis, surpassing hepatitis C in many regions.

Sustained hyperglycemia and glomerular hypertension activate TGF-β in podocytes and mesangial cells, driving glomerulosclerosis and interstitial fibrosis. Together these cause approximately 70% of new dialysis starts in high-income countries.

After infarction, necrotic myocardium is replaced by replacement fibrosis. Chronic hypertension and aortic stenosis drive reactive interstitial fibrosis through angiotensin II and aldosterone-mediated myofibroblast activation.

Systemic sclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, dermatomyositis, and Sjögren syndrome cause lung, skin, and renal fibrosis through chronic autoimmune injury and TGF-β signaling. ILD is the leading cause of death in systemic sclerosis.

Asbestos, silica, coal dust, beryllium, bird and mold antigens (hypersensitivity pneumonitis), and certain drugs (bleomycin, amiodarone, methotrexate, nitrofurantoin) cause inhalational or systemic fibrosis after months to decades of exposure.

Mutations in surfactant protein genes (SFTPC, SFTPA2), telomerase complex genes (TERT, TERC, RTEL1, PARN), and MUC5B promoter variants increase familial pulmonary fibrosis risk by up to 20-fold and underlie 15-20% of IPF cases.

Thoracic or abdominal radiotherapy for malignancy causes dose-dependent pulmonary, mediastinal, or visceral fibrosis months to years after treatment. Fractionation and modern conformal techniques have reduced but not eliminated this risk.

riskFactors.description: Telomere shortening, mitochondrial dysfunction, and reduced epithelial regenerative capacity make older organs more susceptible to fibrotic repair. IPF incidence rises sharply after age 60.
IPF is roughly twice as common in men as women, in part due to historical smoking and occupational exposure patterns; alcohol-related hepatic fibrosis is also more common in men.
Ever-smoking roughly doubles IPF risk; smoking accelerates hepatic fibrosis in hepatitis C and is independently linked to coronary fibrosis and cardiomyopathy progression.
Drives MASH-related hepatic fibrosis; type 2 diabetes accelerates hepatic and renal fibrosis, doubling the rate of progression to advanced disease (Lazarus 2022).

riskFactors.factor: Chronic alcohol use (>40 g/day men, >20 g/day women)
riskFactors.description: Sustained alcohol intake at these thresholds is the largest single driver of alcohol-related hepatic fibrosis worldwide and accelerates fibrosis in any other underlying liver disease.
Untreated chronic infection drives progressive hepatic fibrosis; direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C cure over 95% of cases and can regress fibrosis up to stage F3.
Silica, asbestos, coal dust, hard-metal dust, bird antigens, and indoor mold exposure cause well-documented pulmonary fibroses. Long latency means cases may emerge decades after exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of pulmonary fibrosis or telomere disease
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives have up to 20-fold higher risk; MUC5B rs35705950 minor allele increases IPF risk roughly 6-fold and is present in 35% of cases versus 11% of controls.
Systemic sclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, dermatomyositis/antisynthetase syndrome, and mixed connective tissue disease carry 20-50% lifetime ILD risk and significant renal and dermal fibrosis risk.
Chronic microaspiration is hypothesized to contribute to IPF; observational data support reflux treatment in selected patients, though randomized evidence is limited.

affectedDemographics: IPF prevalence in adults aged 65-74 is approximately 90 per 100,000; below age 50 it is rare (Raghu 2022)
Advanced hepatic fibrosis affects roughly 1.5% of US adults and is rising sharply with the global MASLD epidemic (NHANES 2017-2018)
Asian populations face higher hepatitis B-related fibrosis burden; sub-Saharan African populations are most affected by tropical endomyocardial fibrosis
Diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease across high- and middle-income countries, with 1.5-2x higher progression rates in Black and South Asian populations
Familial pulmonary fibrosis (≥2 affected first-degree relatives) accounts for roughly 5-15% of all IPF cases
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of fibrosis follows a sequence of clinical assessment, imaging or non-invasive scoring, and tissue confirmation when needed. In suspected pulmonary fibrosis, high-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest is the central test: a definite usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern — subpleural, basal-predominant reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis — supports IPF diagnosis without biopsy under the 2022 ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT guideline (Raghu 2022). Pulmonary function tests show a restrictive defect with reduced FVC and DLCO. Multidisciplinary discussion among pulmonology, thoracic radiology, and pathology is the formal gold standard. In hepatic fibrosis, non-invasive tests have largely displaced routine biopsy: transient elastography (FibroScan) with cutoffs of around 8 kPa for significant fibrosis and 12-15 kPa for advanced fibrosis, combined with serum scores such as FIB-4 (cutoffs 1.3 and 2.67) and ELF, achieve over 85% accuracy versus biopsy for ruling out advanced fibrosis. MR elastography is the most accurate non-invasive test (AUC 0.92-0.96) where available. Liver biopsy with METAVIR staging remains the gold standard for ambiguous cases and treatment trials. Renal fibrosis is diagnosed on kidney biopsy with semi-quantitative interstitial fibrosis tubular atrophy (IFTA) scoring, supplemented by eGFR trajectory and proteinuria. Cardiac fibrosis is detected by cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement for replacement fibrosis and T1 mapping with extracellular volume fraction for diffuse interstitial fibrosis. Across all organs, a precise etiologic workup — serology for connective tissue disease, hypersensitivity panel, viral hepatitis screen, autoantibodies, occupational history — is essential, because treatment depends on the cause more than the scar.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Central test for pulmonary fibrosis. A definite UIP pattern — subpleural, basal reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis — supports an IPF diagnosis without biopsy under ATS/ERS 2022 criteria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin-section non-contrast CT lasting under 10 minutes. Images are acquired in inspiration and expiration; you will be asked to hold your breath for 5-10 seconds at a time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies severity and tracks progression. A restrictive defect (reduced FVC with preserved FEV1/FVC ratio) and reduced DLCO are typical; a 10% decline in FVC over 12 months predicts mortality in IPF.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You sit in a sealed booth and perform a series of breathing maneuvers into a mouthpiece, lasting 30-45 minutes. The test is effort-dependent; a trained technician coaches you through each maneuver.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive measurement of liver stiffness in kilopascals. Values around 8 kPa suggest significant fibrosis, 10-12 kPa advanced fibrosis, and over 15 kPa cirrhosis in MASLD; thresholds differ slightly by etiology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe is placed on the right flank between the ribs while you lie on your back. The scan takes 5-10 minutes, is painless, and produces an immediate kilopascal score.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Inexpensive blood-based scores that combine age, AST, ALT, platelet count, and other variables to risk-stratify liver fibrosis. FIB-4 below 1.3 effectively rules out advanced fibrosis; above 2.67 prompts elastography or specialist referral.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw with results from routine labs; scores are calculated automatically or via online calculators. No additional patient burden.

diagnosticTests.test: MR elastography (liver) or cardiac MRI with T1 mapping
diagnosticTests.purpose: MR elastography is the most accurate non-invasive liver fibrosis test (AUC 0.92-0.96). Cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement detects replacement fibrosis; T1 mapping quantifies diffuse interstitial fibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute MRI scan with gadolinium contrast (unless contraindicated). You lie still in the scanner and follow breath-hold instructions; tight-fitting coil placement over the chest or abdomen is standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard when non-invasive tests are equivocal or for treatment trials. Surgical lung biopsy or transbronchial cryobiopsy for ILD; percutaneous liver biopsy with METAVIR staging; kidney biopsy with IFTA scoring; endomyocardial biopsy in selected cardiac cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthesia with light sedation or full anesthesia depending on organ. Same-day discharge for percutaneous procedures; overnight observation for transbronchial or endomyocardial biopsies. Pathology results in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the cause of fibrosis: ANA, anti-CCP, myositis panel, scleroderma antibodies (anti-Scl-70, anti-centromere), hepatitis B and C, HIV, and IgG4 levels. Treatment depends on identifying a reversible driver.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; turnaround 3-10 days depending on the assay panel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used selectively in suspected hypersensitivity pneumonitis (lymphocytic alveolitis), eosinophilic lung disease, or infection mimicking ILD. Not required for typical IPF.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed during bronchoscopy under sedation. The bronchoscope wedges in a peripheral airway, saline is instilled and aspirated, and fluid is sent for cell count, microbiology, and cytology. Procedure takes 30-45 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of fibrosis is built around three pillars: remove or treat the inciting injury, deploy antifibrotic therapy where licensed, and replace the organ when failure becomes irreversible. The 2022 ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT guideline conditionally recommends both pirfenidone and nintedanib for IPF, the only antifibrotics with proven mortality and FVC-decline benefit. Nintedanib has also been licensed (since 2020) for progressive pulmonary fibrosis of any cause based on the INBUILD trial (Flaherty 2019) and for systemic sclerosis-associated ILD based on SENSCIS (Distler 2019). For hepatic fibrosis, the central principle is etiologic control: direct-acting antivirals cure over 95% of hepatitis C and can regress fibrosis up to stage F3; nucleoside analogs (entecavir, tenofovir) suppress hepatitis B and stabilize or reverse fibrosis in over 70% of long-term users; weight loss of 7-10% in MASH reduces fibrosis stage in roughly 40% of patients; resmetirom (Rezdiffra) became the first FDA-approved drug specifically for MASH-related fibrosis in March 2024. Renal fibrosis is managed by ACE inhibitors or ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors (which slow CKD progression by 35-44% in DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY), tight blood-pressure control under 130/80, and finerenone in diabetic kidney disease (FIDELIO-DKD trial). Cardiac fibrosis treatment targets the underlying driver: renin-angiotensin-aldosterone blockade, SGLT2 inhibitors, sacubitril-valsartan, and disease-specific therapy in amyloidosis (tafamidis). Across all forms, smoking cessation, immunization (influenza, pneumococcal, SARS-CoV-2), pulmonary rehabilitation or cardiac rehabilitation, and treatment of comorbidities (reflux, sleep apnea, depression) measurably improve outcomes. End-stage organ fibrosis (advanced IPF, decompensated cirrhosis, dialysis-dependent CKD, refractory heart failure) is managed by transplantation; lung transplantation gives a 5-year survival of roughly 55% post-IPF, and liver transplantation achieves over 75% 5-year survival in well-selected fibrotic cirrhosis recipients.

medicalTreatments.name: Pirfenidone (titrate to 2403 mg/day in three divided doses with food)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic agent that inhibits TGF-β-mediated collagen production. Licensed for IPF based on CAPACITY (Noble 2011) and ASCEND (King 2014) trials. Common adverse effects include rash, photosensitivity, gastrointestinal upset, and reversible transaminase elevation — monitor LFTs monthly for the first six months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces annual FVC decline by approximately 50% versus placebo; pooled analyses show a 48% relative reduction in all-cause mortality at 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Nintedanib (150 mg twice daily with food)
medicalTreatments.description: Intracellular tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting PDGFR, VEGFR, and FGFR pathways. Licensed for IPF, progressive pulmonary fibrosis (INBUILD), and systemic sclerosis-associated ILD (SENSCIS). Diarrhea is the predominant side effect (62% of patients); manage with loperamide and dose reduction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces annual FVC decline by approximately 50% in IPF and 57% in progressive pulmonary fibrosis; signal toward reduced acute exacerbations in INPULSIS pooled analysis.

medicalTreatments.name: Direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir)
medicalTreatments.description: Pan-genotypic regimens given for 8-12 weeks cure hepatitis C in over 95% of patients. Fibrosis regression is documented up to stage F3; patients with cirrhosis still require lifelong surveillance for hepatocellular carcinoma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained virological response in 95-99% of treated patients; histological fibrosis improvement in roughly 50% by 5 years post-cure.

medicalTreatments.name: Tenofovir or entecavir for chronic hepatitis B
medicalTreatments.description: First-line nucleoside/nucleotide analogs that suppress hepatitis B viral replication. Long-term suppression stabilizes or reverses fibrosis in over 70% of compliant patients; treatment is typically indefinite in established cirrhosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Virologic suppression in over 95% by 5 years; fibrosis regression on biopsy in 71% (Marcellin 2013) including 41% of cirrhotic patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Resmetirom (Rezdiffra, 80-100 mg daily based on weight)
medicalTreatments.description: Liver-directed thyroid hormone receptor-β agonist. FDA approved March 2024 for adults with non-cirrhotic MASH and F2-F3 fibrosis based on MAESTRO-NASH (Harrison 2024). First drug specifically approved for MASH fibrosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: MAESTRO-NASH: fibrosis improvement ≥1 stage without MASH worsening achieved in 24-26% versus 14% placebo at 52 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, empagliflozin 10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Originally developed for type 2 diabetes; now first-line for slowing renal fibrosis progression in CKD with or without diabetes, and for heart failure with preserved or reduced ejection fraction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: DAPA-CKD reduced sustained eGFR decline by 39% and renal failure by 44%; EMPA-KIDNEY reduced kidney disease progression by 28%.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers
medicalTreatments.description: Block the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, reducing TGF-β signaling in kidney and heart. First-line in diabetic and hypertensive nephropathy, hypertensive heart disease, and post-MI remodeling.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce proteinuria by 30-40% and slow eGFR decline by approximately 30% in diabetic kidney disease; reduce all-cause mortality after MI by 20-26%.

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide for connective tissue disease ILD
medicalTreatments.description: Used in scleroderma-ILD and other CTD-ILDs. Mycophenolate 1.5 g twice daily is now preferred over cyclophosphamide based on SLS II non-inferiority (Tashkin 2016) and better tolerability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest FVC improvement (1-2% absolute) over 12-24 months; greater benefit in early aggressive disease. Often combined with nintedanib in scleroderma-ILD.

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for end-stage pulmonary fibrosis with progressive respiratory failure despite antifibrotics. Single-lung or bilateral; bilateral is preferred in younger candidates. Listed under the Lung Allocation Score in the US.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 55% 5-year survival post-IPF lung transplant; 10-year survival 30-35% (ISHLT registry).

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: For decompensated cirrhosis with MELD score ≥15, refractory ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, or hepatocellular carcinoma within Milan criteria. Living-donor and deceased-donor options.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Over 75% 5-year patient survival; 90% one-year survival in well-selected fibrotic cirrhosis recipients (OPTN data).

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Best long-term option for end-stage CKD from fibrotic kidney disease. Pre-emptive (before dialysis) transplant gives superior outcomes; living-donor grafts outperform deceased-donor.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 95% one-year and 80% five-year graft survival for living-donor transplants.

surgicalOptions.name: Heart transplantation or ventricular assist device
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for advanced cardiac fibrosis with refractory heart failure (NYHA IV) despite optimal medical therapy. Mechanical support increasingly used as destination therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 85% one-year and 75% five-year survival for heart transplant; LVAD survival continues to improve with newer continuous-flow devices.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Supervised exercise and education program shown in randomized trials to improve 6-minute walk distance by approximately 30 meters and dyspnea scores in IPF (Dowman 2014 Cochrane review). Now standard alongside antifibrotic therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean diet with structured weight loss for MASH
alternativeTreatments.description: Weight loss of 7-10% achieved through a Mediterranean-pattern diet and physical activity reduces fibrosis stage in roughly 40% of MASH patients (Vilar-Gomez 2015). Endorsed by AASLD 2023 guidance.

alternativeTreatments.name: Long-term oxygen therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: For pulmonary fibrosis with resting hypoxemia (SpO2 ≤88% at rest or with exertion). Improves exertional capacity and quality of life, though mortality benefit is more established in COPD than IPF.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to pulmonology with an interstitial lung disease focus for any suspected pulmonary fibrosis, ideally before HRCT pattern is assigned, because multidisciplinary discussion changes diagnosis in roughly 25% of cases (Walsh 2016). Refer to hepatology for FIB-4 above 2.67, transient elastography above 8 kPa, or unexplained ALT elevation; to nephrology for eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m² sustained over three months or proteinuria; and to cardiology for unexplained heart failure with preserved ejection fraction or late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI. Early specialist input changes outcomes — IPF patients seen at expert centers within 6 months of symptom onset have lower mortality than those referred later (Lamas 2011).
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in the relevant specialty plus formal ILD, hepatology, or advanced heart failure training. Ask whether the practice runs a multidisciplinary discussion meeting that includes thoracic radiology and pathology for ILD, or a hepatology MDT for liver fibrosis. Confirm access to advanced imaging (HRCT with thin-section reconstructions, MR elastography, cardiac MRI), antifibrotic medications, and transplant evaluation. For systemic sclerosis or autoimmune-driven fibrosis, choose a center with combined rheumatology and pulmonology clinics. Continuity of care over years matters more than single-visit reputation in chronic fibrotic disease.
hospitalCriteria: Designated ILD center with multidisciplinary discussion that includes thoracic radiology and pulmonary pathology
On-site transient elastography and MR elastography for liver fibrosis staging
Cardiac MRI with T1 mapping and late gadolinium enhancement protocols
Lung, liver, kidney, or heart transplant evaluation program with documented outcomes
Access to clinical trials of next-generation antifibrotics (resmetirom, FGF21 analogs, GLP-1/glucagon dual agonists)
keyFacilities: National Jewish Health, Denver — long-standing IPF and ILD program
Royal Brompton Hospital ILD Unit, London
preventionStrategies: Quit smoking and avoid second-hand smoke at all ages — reduces IPF risk, slows hepatic and renal fibrosis progression, and lowers cardiovascular fibrosis risk
Vaccinate against hepatitis B in childhood and adult risk groups; screen and treat hepatitis C with direct-acting antivirals to prevent progression to cirrhosis
Maintain a body-mass index below 30 and waist circumference under 94 cm (men) or 80 cm (women) to prevent MASH-related hepatic and CKD fibrosis
Treat blood pressure to under 130/80 mmHg and HbA1c to individualized targets to prevent renal and cardiac fibrosis
Use respiratory protection in occupations with silica, asbestos, coal, hard-metal, bird, or mold exposure; comply with national occupational exposure limits
Limit alcohol to under 14 units per week and avoid binge patterns, particularly in those with any pre-existing liver condition
lifestyleModifications: Adopt a Mediterranean-style eating pattern that emphasizes vegetables, legumes, nuts, olive oil, oily fish, and limited red and processed meat
Perform supervised aerobic and resistance exercise 3-5 times weekly, with intensity tailored by your specialist team — pulmonary or cardiac rehabilitation programs are funded in most countries
Stop smoking using a combination of behavioral support and pharmacotherapy (varenicline, bupropion, or nicotine replacement); abstain from cannabis smoke
Screen and treat gastroesophageal reflux disease with proton pump inhibitors and posture measures, especially in established pulmonary fibrosis
Achieve and maintain 7-10% weight loss if overweight or obese, using validated behavioral programs, GLP-1 receptor agonists, or bariatric surgery in selected cases
Treat obstructive sleep apnea with CPAP — improves cardiac fibrosis trajectory and pulmonary hypertension control
Receive annual influenza, age-appropriate pneumococcal, and updated COVID-19 vaccinations — acute infection can precipitate exacerbations and accelerate fibrosis
Address mental health — depression and anxiety are common in chronic organ fibrosis and worsen adherence; CBT and SSRIs are effective and safe

recommended: Mediterranean diet with high vegetable, legume, nut, and olive oil intake — reduces MASH fibrosis progression
Coffee, 2-3 cups daily — associated with reduced hepatic fibrosis progression in MASLD and hepatitis C cohorts
Oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 intake — supports cardiovascular and metabolic health
Whole grains and dietary fiber over refined carbohydrates — lowers insulin resistance and hepatic fat
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to preserve muscle mass, with plant-based sources prioritized in renal fibrosis
Hydration with water; restrict sodium to under 2 g/day in cirrhosis with ascites or heart failure with fibrotic remodeling
avoid: Heavy alcohol intake — the single most modifiable accelerator of hepatic fibrosis at any stage
Sugar-sweetened beverages and high-fructose corn syrup — directly drive hepatic de novo lipogenesis and MASH
Ultra-processed foods and high red/processed meat intake — associated with progression of MASLD, CKD, and cardiovascular fibrosis
Excess sodium (over 5 g/day) — worsens hypertension-driven cardiac and renal fibrosis
Unregulated herbal supplements (kava, comfrey, black cohosh, high-dose green tea extract) — documented hepatotoxicity
Crash diets and prolonged fasting in established cirrhosis — trigger catabolism and worsen sarcopenia

exerciseGuidelines: For pulmonary fibrosis, supervised pulmonary rehabilitation with 30-45 minutes of aerobic exercise plus resistance training 3 times weekly improves walk distance and quality of life. In hepatic fibrosis without decompensation, combine 150-300 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week with 2 resistance sessions to support weight loss and reduce hepatic fat. In renal and cardiac fibrosis, individualized cardiac rehabilitation under ECG monitoring is appropriate, avoiding heavy isometric loads in severe left-ventricular dysfunction. Use oxygen during exertion if SpO2 drops below 88%.
dailyManagement: Take antifibrotic medication with food at the prescribed times — missed doses meaningfully reduce efficacy over 12 months
Track symptoms (dyspnea, weight, edema, abdominal girth) and home spirometry where supplied; share trends with your clinician at each visit
Monitor blood pressure at home twice weekly in renal or cardiac fibrosis; aim under 130/80 mmHg
Schedule routine bloods every 3 months — liver enzymes for antifibrotics and hepatic disease, eGFR and electrolytes for renal disease, NT-proBNP for cardiac fibrosis
Carry a medical alert card listing diagnosis, antifibrotic regimen, oxygen requirement, and transplant status if applicable
Maintain a written acute exacerbation plan with rescue medications and emergency contact numbers
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the organ scarred, the stage at diagnosis, and the inciting injury. Untreated IPF has a median survival of 3-5 years from diagnosis; on pirfenidone or nintedanib, mortality at one year falls by roughly 48% in pooled analyses, and many patients live 7-10 years with stable disease. Hepatic fibrosis is the best example of reversibility: hepatitis C cure regresses fibrosis up to stage F3 in roughly half of patients; abstinence-driven alcohol fibrosis regresses in over 60% at five years; weight loss reduces MASH fibrosis stage in 40%. Once cirrhosis develops, structural reversal is rare but decompensation risk falls sharply with etiologic control. Diabetic and hypertensive nephropathy with sustained SGLT2 inhibitor and ACE/ARB therapy reduces progression to dialysis by 35-44%; the residual renal fibrosis burden defines long-term outcome. Cardiac fibrosis quantified on MRI predicts heart failure and arrhythmic death — high late-gadolinium-enhancement burden roughly doubles 5-year mortality. Across all organs, transplant-eligible patients reaching transplant have 55-90% 5-year survival depending on the organ. The strongest single prognostic predictor is whether the inciting injury is identified and treated.
recoveryTimeline: Antifibrotic effects on lung function are measurable from 6 months and clearest at 12-24 months. Hepatic fibrosis regression after hepatitis C cure progresses over 1-5 years; MASH fibrosis improvement on weight loss or resmetirom is documented at 52 weeks in trial data. Renal fibrosis trajectory is followed over years — SGLT2 inhibitor benefit on eGFR slope is apparent within 6-12 months and persists for the duration of therapy. Cardiac reverse remodeling on guideline-directed medical therapy is typically seen within 6-12 months. Transplant recovery follows organ-specific timelines: 6-12 weeks for return to baseline activity after liver or kidney transplant; 6-12 months for full rehabilitation after lung or heart transplant.
complications: Acute exacerbation of pulmonary fibrosis — sudden worsening over days with diffuse new ground-glass opacification on HRCT; in-hospital mortality approaches 50%
Pulmonary hypertension secondary to fibrosis — develops in 30-50% of advanced IPF and accelerates decline
Variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, ascites, and hepatorenal syndrome in cirrhotic-stage hepatic fibrosis
Hepatocellular carcinoma — annual risk 2-4% in cirrhosis; lifelong 6-monthly ultrasound surveillance is recommended
Progressive end-stage kidney disease requiring dialysis or transplantation
Sudden cardiac death from arrhythmic substrate in cardiac fibrosis, especially post-infarct and amyloid fibrosis
Sarcopenia, malnutrition, and frailty across all advanced fibroses
Depression and anxiety — affect over 30% of patients with chronic organ fibrosis and worsen outcomes

supportResources.name: Pulmonary Fibrosis Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization with information, support groups, and clinical trial directory for pulmonary fibrosis.

supportResources.name: American Liver Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education on hepatic fibrosis, cirrhosis, MASH, and viral hepatitis with regional helplines.

supportResources.name: British Lung Foundation — Asthma + Lung UK
supportResources.description: UK patient information on pulmonary fibrosis with helpline and local support groups.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient information on chronic kidney disease, fibrosis, and transplantation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis (acute and chronic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver; hepatic fibrosis is the scar that develops after sustained hepatitis. ALT and AST rise in active hepatitis but may normalize even as fibrosis advances. FibroScan or biopsy is required to detect fibrosis stage independently of inflammation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scleroderma is an autoimmune disease that causes skin and internal organ fibrosis as part of a broader vascular and immune disorder. Fibrosis is therefore a major manifestation of scleroderma, but most fibrosis worldwide is not scleroderma-related; antibody panels and skin involvement distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is an obstructive lung disease driven by airway inflammation and emphysema; pulmonary fibrosis is a restrictive disease with stiff fibrotic lung. Spirometry distinguishes them: obstruction (low FEV1/FVC) in COPD versus restriction (preserved or high ratio, low FVC) in fibrosis. HRCT findings differ markedly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart Failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heart failure is a clinical syndrome of impaired cardiac output; cardiac fibrosis is one of several mechanisms causing it. Many heart failure patients have fibrosis on cardiac MRI, but heart failure can also arise from valve disease, arrhythmia, or ischemia without dominant fibrosis.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Asbestosis is a specific occupational pulmonary fibrosis caused by inhaled asbestos fibers; shares HRCT features with IPF including subpleural reticulation.

relatedConditions.name: Silicosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Silica-induced pulmonary fibrosis with nodular and progressive massive fibrosis patterns; relevant in mining, construction, and stone-cutting workers.

relatedConditions.name: Farmer's Lung
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis from mold antigen exposure that progresses to fibrosis if exposure continues.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune disease causing skin, lung, kidney, and gut fibrosis; ILD is the leading cause of scleroderma mortality.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis C
relatedConditions.relevance: Leading historical cause of hepatic fibrosis worldwide; direct-acting antivirals now cure over 95% and can regress fibrosis.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Progressive cyst expansion drives interstitial fibrosis and end-stage kidney disease typically between ages 50-70.

faqs.question: Is fibrosis reversible?
faqs.answer: Early fibrosis is often reversible if the cause is removed. Hepatic fibrosis up to stage F3 regresses in around half of hepatitis C patients cured with antivirals and in over 60% of alcohol-related cases with sustained abstinence. Pulmonary fibrosis is not reversed by current drugs, but pirfenidone and nintedanib slow progression by roughly 50%. Cirrhosis (F4) and advanced pulmonary scarring with honeycombing are largely structural and do not reverse, though further damage can be prevented.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of fibrosis?
faqs.answer: It depends on the organ. Worldwide, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is now the leading cause of advanced hepatic fibrosis, surpassing hepatitis C in many regions. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is the most common cause of progressive lung fibrosis, with cigarette smoking and genetic factors as key drivers. Diabetes and hypertension are the leading causes of renal fibrosis. Post-myocardial infarction scarring is the most common cause of cardiac fibrosis.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of pulmonary fibrosis?
faqs.answer: The earliest symptoms are gradual exertional breathlessness and a dry, persistent cough that lasts more than two months. Patients often notice they cannot keep up with peers when climbing stairs or hills. Fine inspiratory crackles on chest auscultation are detectable before significant change on chest X-ray. Anyone over 50 with unexplained dyspnea and a dry cough lasting eight weeks should have a chest HRCT and pulmonary function tests.

faqs.question: How is liver fibrosis measured?
faqs.answer: Liver fibrosis is most commonly measured by transient elastography (FibroScan), which gives a stiffness score in kilopascals. Around 8 kPa suggests significant fibrosis, 10-12 kPa advanced fibrosis, and over 15 kPa cirrhosis in MASLD. Blood scores such as FIB-4 (calculated from age, AST, ALT, and platelets) and ELF are useful screening tools. MR elastography is the most accurate non-invasive test. Liver biopsy with METAVIR staging remains the gold standard when results are ambiguous.

faqs.question: What is the difference between fibrosis and cirrhosis?
faqs.answer: Cirrhosis is the most advanced stage of liver fibrosis — specifically METAVIR stage F4. Fibrosis describes the scarring process at any stage from mild (F1) to severe (F3) to cirrhosis (F4). Early fibrosis can regress with treatment; cirrhosis is largely structural and is associated with portal hypertension, ascites, encephalopathy, and increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma. All cirrhosis is fibrosis, but not all fibrosis is cirrhosis.

faqs.question: Can pulmonary fibrosis be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis with current medications. Pirfenidone and nintedanib slow progression by approximately 50% and reduce one-year mortality, but they do not reverse existing scarring. The only definitive treatment for end-stage pulmonary fibrosis is lung transplantation, which achieves roughly 55% five-year survival. Research into new antifibrotics, including pamrevlumab and FGF21-based agents, continues.

faqs.question: How long do you live with pulmonary fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, median survival in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is 3-5 years from diagnosis. With antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib), pooled trial data show a 48% reduction in one-year mortality, and many patients remain stable for 7-10 years. The trajectory varies widely — some patients decline slowly over a decade, others have rapid progression within 1-2 years. Outcomes are better in younger patients with preserved lung function at diagnosis and access to expert centers.

faqs.question: Are antifibrotic drugs safe to take long term?
faqs.answer: Pirfenidone and nintedanib have over a decade of post-marketing safety data and are considered safe for indefinite use in IPF. The main adverse effects are gastrointestinal upset (especially diarrhea with nintedanib), photosensitivity and rash with pirfenidone, and reversible liver enzyme elevation with both. Monthly liver function monitoring is standard for the first six months. Most discontinuations are for tolerability rather than serious harm, and dose reduction often allows continued therapy.

faqs.question: Does fibrosis run in families?
faqs.answer: Familial pulmonary fibrosis accounts for 5-15% of all IPF cases. Mutations in surfactant protein genes (SFTPC, SFTPA2), telomerase complex genes (TERT, TERC, RTEL1, PARN), and the MUC5B rs35705950 promoter variant increase risk substantially. First-degree relatives of IPF patients have up to 20-fold higher risk. Hereditary causes are less prominent in hepatic, renal, and cardiac fibrosis, where exposure-driven mechanisms dominate.

faqs.question: Can weight loss reverse liver fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes — sustained weight loss of 7-10% reduces fibrosis stage in approximately 40% of MASH patients and resolves steatohepatitis in 65-90% depending on the magnitude of weight loss. A Mediterranean-pattern diet plus 150-300 minutes of weekly aerobic activity is the foundation. GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, tirzepatide) and bariatric surgery achieve larger weight loss and greater fibrosis benefit in trials.

faqs.question: What is the cost of antifibrotic therapy?
faqs.answer: List prices for pirfenidone and nintedanib in the US are roughly USD 100,000-120,000 per year, though most insured patients pay a fraction through commercial insurance or Medicare Part D with manufacturer assistance programs. In Europe and most public systems, both drugs are covered for IPF and (for nintedanib) for progressive pulmonary fibrosis. Generic pirfenidone is available in India at roughly USD 50-100 per month.

faqs.question: How is renal fibrosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Renal fibrosis is suspected from a sustained fall in eGFR or persistent proteinuria over months. Definitive diagnosis is by kidney biopsy with semi-quantitative scoring of interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IFTA). Once IFTA exceeds 50%, progression to kidney failure is near-certain. Non-invasive imaging-based tests are under investigation but not yet standard. eGFR trajectory and proteinuria remain the most useful day-to-day measures.

faqs.question: Can exercise improve fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Exercise does not directly reverse scarring, but supervised pulmonary or cardiac rehabilitation improves walk distance, dyspnea, and quality of life in fibrotic lung and heart disease. Physical activity supports weight loss in MASH-related liver fibrosis, where 7-10% loss can reduce fibrosis stage. In CKD, regular activity slows muscle wasting and helps blood pressure control. Exercise should be prescribed by your specialist team and tailored to organ function.

faqs.question: What is the connection between COVID-19 and fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Severe COVID-19, particularly cases requiring mechanical ventilation, can leave behind post-COVID pulmonary fibrosis in 5-10% of survivors at 12 months on HRCT. Most early changes regress, but a minority show progressive fibrotic ILD. Nintedanib has been studied for progressive post-COVID fibrosis with modest benefit. The phenomenon is most relevant in survivors of severe acute respiratory distress syndrome rather than mild outpatient COVID.

faqs.question: Is fibrosis painful?
faqs.answer: Fibrosis itself is usually not painful — the scar tissue lacks the inflammatory nerve activation of acute injury. Discomfort comes from the consequences: breathlessness in pulmonary fibrosis, abdominal distension and right upper-quadrant ache in hepatic fibrosis, leg swelling in cardiac or renal fibrosis, and skin tightness in dermal fibrosis. Acute exacerbations of pulmonary fibrosis and decompensation of cirrhosis can be acutely distressing rather than painful per se.

faqs.question: What blood tests detect fibrosis?
faqs.answer: There is no single blood test for fibrosis. For liver fibrosis, the FIB-4 score (calculated from age, AST, ALT, and platelet count) and the ELF panel are widely used. For renal fibrosis, eGFR and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio are the standard. Cardiac fibrosis is suggested by elevated NT-proBNP and high-sensitivity troponin alongside imaging. For pulmonary fibrosis, blood tests are mainly used to identify the cause (autoimmune panel, hepatitis screen) rather than the scar itself.

faqs.question: Can stem cell therapy treat fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Stem cell therapy for fibrosis is investigational. Mesenchymal stromal cell trials have shown safety in IPF (AETHER, Glassberg 2017) and modest signals in hepatic fibrosis, but no regulator has approved stem cells for fibrotic disease. Unregulated clinics offering stem cell infusions for fibrosis charge thousands of dollars without trial evidence and carry real risks. Patients considering this should enroll in a registered clinical trial instead.

faqs.question: How often should fibrosis be monitored?
faqs.answer: Pulmonary fibrosis is monitored with pulmonary function tests every 3-6 months and HRCT when there is change. Hepatic fibrosis is monitored with FibroScan and FIB-4 every 6-12 months while stable, and ultrasound every 6 months in cirrhosis to screen for liver cancer. Renal fibrosis is followed with eGFR and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio every 3-6 months. Cardiac fibrosis is reassessed by cardiac MRI or echocardiography every 6-12 months depending on stability.

faqs.question: What is the role of diet in fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Diet matters most in hepatic fibrosis, where a Mediterranean pattern with weight loss reduces MASH fibrosis stage in roughly 40% of patients. Coffee 2-3 cups daily is associated with reduced hepatic fibrosis progression. In renal fibrosis, sodium restriction under 2 g/day and adequate but not excessive protein intake slow progression. Alcohol acceleration of fibrosis is universal and dose-related across organs. Unregulated herbal supplements should be avoided.

metaDescription: Fibrosis: scarring of lung, liver, kidney, heart, or skin. Symptoms, HRCT and FibroScan diagnosis, antifibrotic therapy, reviewed by our medical board.
